title: Burnt Basque cheesecake
subtitle: Baked cream cheese cake with a deliberately caramelised top
excerpt: A Basque-style baked cheesecake cooked at very high heat to produce a deeply caramelised, almost burnt top over a barely set, creamy interior. No biscuit base. No water bath. Made in one bowl and baked at 220°C until the top is dark and the centre trembles. Serves eight to ten.
sourceNotes: Baked cream cheese cakes and curd-based sweet preparations are documented in European domestic cooking from the nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: baked curd and cream preparations are recorded in the chapter on puddings. The technique of baking at high heat to produce a caramelised surface is documented in Spanish regional baking traditions and in European domestic baking manuals of the twentieth century available through the Internet Archive.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made the day before serving; the texture firms and the flavour deepens overnight. Keeps refrigerated for 3 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, upper shelf. The very high heat is intentional — it produces the caramelised top and barely set interior. Do not reduce the temperature

glossaryTerms.definition: The defining feature of a Basque-style cheesecake: a dark, almost burnt surface produced by baking at very high temperature. The sugar in the batter caramelises on contact with intense heat, producing a bitter-sweet crust that contrasts with the soft, barely set interior. The top should be darker than instinct suggests is safe. A pale, uniformly golden surface means the oven was too cool or the bake too short — the interior will be fully set rather than trembling at the centre.

content.text: This is not a cheesecake that needs a water bath, a biscuit base, or careful temperature monitoring. It needs a very hot oven and the confidence to leave it in longer than feels right. The top should be dark, almost burnt. The centre should shake when you move the tin. Both of these things are correct.

Preheat the oven to 220°C fan. Line a 23 cm springform tin with baking paper, pressing it into the base and up the sides with a generous overhang (the paper will crumple and that is expected). Beat the {{cream-cheese}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until smooth. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Pour in the {{double-cream}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} and beat briefly until combined. Fold in the sifted {{plain-flour}}. Pour into the prepared tin. Bake for 25–28 minutes. The top should be deeply dark — this is the
. The centre will tremble when you move the tin. That is correct.

Cool completely in the tin at room temperature — it will collapse and crack slightly as it cools, which is expected. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours before slicing. Serve cold or at cool room temperature.
